event: Early modern humans inhabit the Niah Caves in Sarawak, leaving behind one of Southeast Asia's oldest human skulls (the Deep Skull).
Hoabinhian hunter-gatherer cultures spread across the Malay Peninsula during the late Pleistocene and early Holocene.
Austronesian-speaking peoples migrate southward into the region, introducing agriculture, pottery, and seafaring traditions.
Iron Age communities engage in maritime trade, linking the peninsula to wider Indian Ocean and South China Sea networks.
Indianized trading polities emerge along the peninsula, adopting Hindu-Buddhist culture, Sanskrit, and Indian statecraft.
The early state of Langkasuka is established on the northern Malay Peninsula as a hub of regional commerce.
The Srivijaya maritime empire, based in Sumatra, extends its commercial and political dominance over the Malay Peninsula's coasts.
The Chola dynasty of South India raids Srivijayan ports along the peninsula, weakening Srivijaya's grip on the straits.
The Kedah kingdom converts to Islam, with its ruler taking the title Sultan Mudzafar Shah according to local tradition.
Srivijayan power collapses, fragmenting control of the Strait of Malacca among competing local rulers.
Parameswara founds the Malacca Sultanate, which rapidly grows into the dominant trading port of the region.
Malacca formalizes a tributary relationship with Ming China, hosting visits from the admiral Zheng He's fleets.
Parameswara embraces Islam, accelerating the religion's spread across the Malay world through Malacca's influence.
Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque conquer Malacca, establishing the first European colony in the region.
The Johor Sultanate is founded by descendants of Malacca's royal line, becoming a leading Malay power.
The Dutch East India Company, allied with Johor, captures Malacca from the Portuguese.
Francis Light acquires Penang for the British East India Company, establishing the first British foothold on the peninsula.
Stamford Raffles founds Singapore, drawing trade away from Dutch-held ports.
The Anglo-Dutch Treaty divides the Malay world, assigning the peninsula and Singapore to British influence.
Britain unites Penang, Malacca, and Singapore into the Straits Settlements.
James Brooke becomes the first White Rajah of Sarawak after aiding the Sultan of Brunei against a rebellion.
The Pangkor Treaty introduces the British Residential System into Perak, beginning indirect colonial control of the Malay states.
The British North Borneo Company receives a charter to administer North Borneo (modern Sabah).
Britain organizes Perak, Selangor, Negeri Sembilan, and Pahang into the Federated Malay States.
Siam cedes Kedah, Kelantan, Perlis, and Terengganu to British control under the Anglo-Siamese Treaty.
Japanese forces invade Malaya, beginning a swift campaign down the peninsula during World War II.
Japan completes its occupation of Malaya, Singapore, and Borneo, ending British rule for the war's duration.
British rule is restored after Japan's surrender, amid rising nationalist sentiment.
The United Malays National Organisation (UMNO) is founded to oppose the centralized Malayan Union proposal.
The Federation of Malaya is established, and the Malayan Emergency begins as communist insurgents take up arms.
Malaya holds its first federal elections, won decisively by the Alliance coalition led by Tunku Abdul Rahman.
Malaya gains independence from Britain on 31 August, with Tunku Abdul Rahman as its first prime minister.
The Malayan Emergency is officially declared over after the defeat of the communist insurgency.
Malaysia is formed by uniting Malaya with Singapore, Sarawak, and North Borneo (Sabah), prompting confrontation with Indonesia.
Singapore separates from Malaysia to become an independent republic.
Deadly ethnic riots erupt in Kuala Lumpur on 13 May, leading to a suspension of parliament.
The New Economic Policy is launched to reduce poverty and restructure economic disparities between ethnic groups.
Mahathir Mohamad becomes prime minister, beginning a 22-year tenure marked by rapid industrialization.
Mahathir announces Vision 2020, a plan to make Malaysia a fully developed nation by that year.
The Asian Financial Crisis batters Malaysia's economy, prompting capital controls and a currency peg.
Kuala Lumpur opens the Petronas Twin Towers, then the world's tallest buildings, and hosts the Commonwealth Games.
Abdullah Ahmad Badawi succeeds Mahathir as prime minister, ending Mahathir's long premiership.
The ruling Barisan Nasional coalition loses its two-thirds parliamentary majority in a landmark general election.
Najib Razak becomes prime minister and launches the '1Malaysia' national unity campaign.
Malaysia Airlines suffers two disasters as Flight MH370 vanishes and Flight MH17 is shot down over Ukraine.
The Pakatan Harapan coalition wins power, ending six decades of Barisan Nasional rule, with Mahathir returning as prime minister.
The 'Sheraton Move' political crisis collapses the Pakatan Harapan government, and the COVID-19 pandemic triggers nationwide lockdowns.
Anwar Ibrahim becomes prime minister after inconclusive elections, ending decades of political struggle.
